speaker: interviewer
text: The date is November 17. David, cut for a minute. The date is November 17, 1995. Survivor is Magda Herzberger. Her last name at birth was Moses. The interviewer is Louise Bobrow. The city is Fountain Hills in the state of Arizona. And the country is the United States of America. And the language is in English. My name is Louise Bobrow. Today's date is November 17, 1995. I'm conducting an interview with Magda Herzberger. The interview is being conducted in Fountain Hills, Arizona. Good afternoon. What is your complete name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My complete name is Magdalena Herzberger, but everyone calls me Magda.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you spell that for me, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: M-A-G-D-A-L-E-N-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: M-- [LAUGHS] I almost said something wrong. Herzberger. And it's H-E-R-Z-B-E-R-G-E-R. I almost used my maiden name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, that's my next question. What was your maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My maiden name was Moses.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: M-O-S-E-S.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1926, February 20.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your present age?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, 69. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Romania in the city of Cluj.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you spell that please?

speaker: interviewee
text: City of Cluj, I spell that first, C-L-U-J. And Romania, it's-- you know how you spell Romania, R-U-M-A-N-I-A. Or, some-- some people spell it with R-O, Romania, R-O-M-A-N-I-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little bit of about Cluj as you were growing up. What-- how big was the city first of all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Cluj was close to about 150,000, the population. And it was a historical city. Also, it was the capital of Transylvania. It has the best university, the best medical school. Actually, everyone from Romania wanted to-- to study from in that medical school. It was a beautiful city. And I could compare it to-- if I think of the United States what city would be compared to Cluj would be Madison. But, actually, the climate was different.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the population of Jews at the time that you're growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'm not sure, 100% sure, what the population was-- 9,000 or 10,000 or more, I-- I'm not so sure. It-- it wasn't exactly like now where living in the United States and you know everything, you know? The surveys were not exactly like now. Here, we go really back in time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it an agricultural city or a commercial type of city?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was more of a cultural city I would say.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of culture did they have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had opera houses. We had-- actually, we had one opera house. It was beautiful. And we had theater. And there were historical sites. And we have a university and medical school. And also, we had beautiful libraries. And it very much-- the accent was very much on culture and education. We had the best gymnasiums. You know, it was different educational system than in the United States. It was an elite city, I would say, in that sense. And agriculture was around the city, you know, in the-- in the-- in the-- they had-- you had farms. But not, naturally, in the city itself, was-- had a different spirit.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the home in which you grew up in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We-- well, I can't remember quite well the home where I was a baby, you know. Because it happened so that we moved. And there-- I would say if you ask me my earliest memories, when I was about three years old and four years old, we lived at the premises of a large engine factory, called [NON-ENGLISH]. And my father was the manager of that factory. And we had a beautiful home at our disposal. Those were the best times. Naturally, the-- the factory was-- had gates that nobody could enter, you know. But it was a-- it was a nice home. And the premises were quite large. And I remember-- I remember our home. And, you know, the rooms were large and the high ceiling. And the house was pretty big. And I remember when I was a child, you know, I always was running in the bedroom that my parents had. And I was chased out. And I had to go into my room. And then I remember that we had a sort of an open-- a closed patio. And my mother loved plants. We always had plants. They were there in winter. She would take them in. And then I remember we-- that on the premises there were three dogs. And I even remember their names. One was called Albo, that was a German Shepherd. Albo, in Romanian, albo means white. And then Lupo, which means a special German Shepherd, you know, which is like a wolf, a little bit of wolf look. [INAUDIBLE] Lupe mean wolf, lupo. And then other-- the other dog was called Caesar. And he was a true leader. And you know, right now, I'm always afraid of dogs, because we used to bicycling, bicycle tours. And the dogs were running after our bicycle. But at age three, I loved those dogs. And, you know, then when I was at the kindergarten age, the-- my mother knew when I come home, because the Albo, Lupo, and Caesar were waiting by the gate. Then they would-- they would-- they would come with me to the house. They would accompany me to the house. I loved those dogs. And then I would give them-- my would stuffed cabbage-- I would steal stuffed cabbage to give to Caesar. Because he was my favorite. But then I have also other memories. At that time, my uncle had a-- had-- had a-- fencing-- a room there where he was living in-- on those premises. And he had a room where he was teaching fencing. And I will-- if you ask me, I will talk to you about that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me, before we get onto that, tell me a little bit about your parents. Your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Herman, which means-- you know what Herman means? Herman, if you have on the Hebrew name is Tzvi. And it means deer.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his last name was Moses?

speaker: interviewee
text: Moses, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a manager of a large engine factory. That-- these were the times I was talking to you previously when I was a child. But then when I got to the-- to-- to the first elementary school, already the Jewish persecution was starting. And the best times when I was a real young child. And so then he could not be anymore manager, because no Jews were allowed to be managers. And then he-- he was accountant, a chief accountant. Then he couldn't be any more chief accountant. Then he was just an accountant. And then when the times were bad that he had to take three jobs in order that we should survive. It was really sad. But the times, you know, I'm talking about when I was 3, 4, 5, 6 years old, those were good times. And especially when I was even 3 or 4 or 5, those were better time-- I mean, there still good times for Jewish people. But then later on things change. And then they changed drastically.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to go on to that also. Tell me about your mother. Her first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her first name is Serena.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: And her maiden name Daszkal. And D-A-S-Z-- you know, in Hungarian, that's a Hungarian name. And in Hungarian, if you have S and Z, you read s, Daszkal. And then it's K-A-L.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she have an occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my mother-- see, my mother wanted to help my father. She was interested in accounting. But at that time, there were many chores, a different lifestyle, you know, than-- than here. And my father said, well, I earn enough. I don't want you to work. You can take care of-- of, you know, I was an only child. And-- and at that time, we didn't have washing machines. And we didn't have any-- any dryers. And there was a lady who came to wash. Everything had to be hand made. My mother had to put all the preserves. We had a pantry room.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you know how much work it was? I mean, I had to participate. It wasn't, you know, that just my mother does. I had my own responsibilities.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one of them-- I sometimes-- I loved the summer, and I didn't. Because there was a tomato season. And then there was a strawberry season. And every time, we had to carry things from the market. It was-- it was such as a chore. But my mother was very good in crafts. And at one time, she was teaching crafts in Israel, when she was in Israel. I encouraged her. She was teaching crafts at the women's club. But she always-- she wanted-- my mother was a very outgoing person, you know. And-- but then she helped my grandmother in the business. So my father couldn't just stop her doing nothing in that sense.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language was mainly spoken in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my first language-- and I carry this accent, which you hear today-- was Hungarian. Because my parents, my grandparents, and my great grandparents were-- at that time when they were young, that whole territory, Transylvania, and this city of Cluj where most of my family was born, was a part of the Austro-Hungarian Empire.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my parents spoke well German, too, because of that. But Hungarian was my first language. And I think that any language-- I speak other languages-- but the accent is definitely a Hungarian in all the languages that I mastered after that. Could you describe for me how some of the Jewish holidays were celebrated in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this--

speaker: interviewer
text: Start with Shabbat. What do you remember about Shabbat?

speaker: interviewee
text: I tell you, just with the subject, I get very emotional. Because I don't think that that I will ever see celebrations that we experience. Forgive me, but this is a very weak point here. Because, you know, in the camps, when I was a prisoner, I had to-- I had to take my strengths and I took my strengths from the beautiful celebrations in our holidays. I remember the situation was terrible. I never knew if I lived one day or another if I live at the end of the day and what's going to happen to me. And then I said to myself, well, I have to live. I have to make it. Because I want to relive our beautiful holidays, our beautiful customs. I want to see my parents. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was tradition. You know, tradition is something very important. And I grew up in Orthodox Jewish tradition. You know, is speaking about tradition. Well, I do believe in tradition. I-- I-- I-- I am a proud to be at Jew. And I almost died. And I grew up with a Jewish holidays and celebrations.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Shabbat, you said. Well, the Shabbat was beautiful, because we already started on Friday. My mother worked-- Friday was the work, the-- the day, you know, I didn't like. Because it was work and work, Friday, cooking and preparing the dinners and cakes. My mother was a very good cook. Everything was prepared on Friday.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so on Friday night, then my mother lighted the candles. And we went to the temple. Now, there were two temples. We had the Orthodox and the Neolog temple. On Friday night, sometimes we like to go to the Neolog temple, because my religion teacher was Rabbi Wainderberg. And believe it or not, he returned. He's alive. And I have a memorial book. And I'm going to show you his picture, the picture of Rabbi Wainderberg. And-- so, it was-- Rabbi Wainderberg was very special. That's why our Friday nights were so special. Because he had an operatic voice. And I don't think I will ever experience a cantor or rabbi, who has tremendous voice. He could have been an opera singer. But that's what he wanted to do. And so he was singing Friday night like-- this-- this-- the Neolog temple, we called it Neolog-- the temple was like a-- like a the theater with a balcony. And at that time, the girls were up and the women, and the men were down. And he was a very handsome man, you know. And many girls had a crush on him, too. But then naturally, we had a Friday night dinner. And my mother made the best fish. And [SOBBING] I wish she would be alive. Forgive me, but I lost her last year. And when you talk about this, I remember the times, you know, when I can see her lighting the candles and-- and-- and the beautiful things on the table and the nice cakes that she made. My mother was a great cook. And that was-- that was the Shabbat, you know And on the-- on the Shabbat, we got together with the family. We were a large family, you see. I have cousins, many cousins. And my mother had sisters. And-- and Saturday was a time when, you know, after lunch a family got together. Or, we had lunch together. My grandparents had a big house at that time. My grandfather was a businessman. And then sometimes we will go all there, you know. And it was-- it was specials. Our holidays were-- were celebrated in a very special way. And I remember-- I remember the Passover. Now, you're not good to see Passover celebrated like in our home. Maybe you do. But my father put on his white robe. And he put the pillows, bright pillows of the chair. And then we had the Haggadah, everyone following all the ritual. And naturally, the [NON-ENGLISH], it's called, you know, the matzoh is put for me to find. And-- and-- and I wanted to look where he put it. And-- and I a little bit opening my eyes. And my father, I think, wanted to ignore that, so that I'm not too unhappy to find it. We went to the temple. Like Yom Kippur, the Day of Atonement, we were the whole day in the temple. We were fasting. And we had the feast in the evening. And we went to the Orthodox temple for-- for services. And then New Year's-- Passover, my mother was working. My god, every single crumb had to be taking care of, so that you don't have anything. The new dishes were taken in for Passover dishes. Everything was sterilized. And then we had the Passover matzoh and all the Passover flour, I mean, Passover dishes. I-- you know, I have a news clipping here. I will show you maybe later on. It's a picture of me and my mother. And the interviewer came at Passover. And she was preparing the matzoh balls, you know. Well, and I-- I feel that I was fortunate to grow up, you know. And-- also Sukkot, we-- we had a-- we had a whole ritual. You know, usually my grandfather, my grandmother had to attend there. And these were nice memories that kept me alive the camps. Believe me, it did. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's-- you know, forgive me that I get the emotional. But that's natural, you know. Not only that they kept me alive in the camps, but if I want to think back, you know, of some wonderful things in my life, they still give me enjoyment. Although I'm getting older now, you know. But-- but I grew up-- once you grew up with that, it stays with you for you a lifetime. I hope I didn't talk too much. But the subject was very-- very close to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What schools did you attend?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I have to tell me about-- about our system, our school system, before you and I can mention that. I had-- in our school system, we had four elementary schools. And then we had eight gymnasiums. Now, the gymnasium incorporated really four years of college. Because at the end of your fourth years of gymnasium, you had to decide what profession you are going to select. And if you wanted to go to medical school, then you had to take sciences. And if you wanted to go to literature, naturally you have to take arts, arts classes. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so I had the four elementary schools. I had my eight gymnasium. Then after the eight gymnasium, you have a very strict exam, called Baccalaureate. And you have to master all the subjects that you had through the eight years. It's the nightmare of everyone. And it's a big test, which can last several days, and oral and written for every subject. Now, after you get-- you-- you pass this test-- you have to pass this test-- then you get the Bachelor of Science or Bachelor of Art degree. And now, if you wanted to go to the university-- suppose you wanted to be an engineer or you wanted to go into arts, you didn't need anything else except this and you automatically registered. If you wanted to go to medical school, which was my dream, you couldn't. Because you have to pass an entrance examination, you know, to the medical school. So I-- I got the entrance examination. And I continued my studies after I came back from the camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I have-- I was in my second year of medical school when we emigrated. And it was my dream, you know. You know how you have a dream, and then finally you see the dream materializing? And then just like in the dream, it vanishes. Because you cannot hang onto it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's what happened to me. I was very happy. I was so happy after I came back from the camps. You know, we were totally-- totally ruined economically. And my mother had a job. I mean, wasn't [? plain, ?] a restaurant, a cashier in a restaurant. But we could have-- fortunately, we could have free food, you know. And I-- I wanted to continue my studies. So when we left, in 1947, end of 1947, we left Romania after I returned, you know. It's a long story. But you maybe ask questions about that. And I was my second year of medical school.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in other words, that's-- that's where I stopped my education. But I never stopped, you know, truly, my education, but my formal, I would say, formal education. Did you have many non-Jewish friends growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, not many. You know, there was-- there was a reason why. Because when I got into the gymnasium, I mean, I-- I experienced Jewish persecution ever since I entered the first the gymnasium, you know. Because it was-- you know, when I started even my elementary school, the things change. You know, it was in the '30s. And already the Nazis were forming and developing, you know. And it was affecting already. And I can talk to you about it, you know, if you--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. I was going to ask you how Hitler's rise to power in 1933 began to change your life.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, this-- this really changed drastically our lives. What happened-- see in 1937, I was born in 1926. So in-- you know, I-- I went to school when I was seven years old. Because that's-- that-- that was the rule there, you know, that you can go to school until you are seven. So seven years old I entered first elementary school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, by the time in the '30s that I went to the gymnasium, the Nazis, you know, was growing, see? And it affected. Why did it affect? You know, I have to tell you what happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, I lived in northern part of Transylvania. And northern part of Transylvania at the time that I got to school, even first elementary school, belonged to Romania. And actually, the whole Transylvania belonged to Romania. My-- my husband is from southern part of Transylvania. See, in the southern part of Transylvania, the Jewish people were taken to forced labor camps. And-- and but in northern part of Transylvania, you know, then it became Hungary in 1940. See, in 1940 were very hard times. But even before that, the all the Jews from northern part of Transylvania, which was Hungary in 1944, were taken out to the German concentration camps, the sick people, everyone. There was no-- there was no Jew left in-- in-- except some people who maybe who were in hiding, you know. But they pulled them out from the hospitals, from everywhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened in which way it affected, because the anti-Semitic laws came into function, you know. And what happened though, you see, after-- before World War I, the whole Transylvania, as I mentioned to you, was part of Austro-Hungarian empire.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, what happened, that after the end of First World War in 1920 was signed a treaty, which was called a Trianon Treaty. And after this treaty, naturally, Hungary was practically, parts of Hungary were parceled out. And they were parceled out to Czechoslovakia, to-- and and whole Transylvania, you know, went to Romania. And the Hungarians resented that. So, suddenly, the whole of Transylvania in 1920 became Romania. Now, when I started school, I had to go to Romanian schools. Although, I didn't speak a single word of Hungarian-- Romanian, because we were speaking Hungarian. So-- So what happened then, in 19-- the Hungarians resented the loss of Transylvania. It was a bait for them from Hitler to tell them, like you put the little cheese to the mouse. If you join me and you agree with my policies you are going to get back Transylvania. And the Hungarians joined Hitler, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, suddenly, the government was toward Nazis and fascist. And, naturally, this affected us. And why did they join? There was a political and economic difficulty, you know. And so they joined them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then anti-Jewish laws came gradually in function. I told you my father, they attacked already the people who were in important positions. And then only a limited number of Jewish students were accepted the gymnasium. And you had an-- an exam at the end of the fourth elementary school. If you didn't score high, if you were a Jew, you never could get your education. When you were in a school, they were accepted seven students for a classroom. And this is when I really experienced for the first time real Jewish persecution, not only discrimination. Because in school, for instance, we were asked every single day, the Jewish students, who are the Jewish students, so that we can be prosecuted. If didn't know-- if, for instance, the Romanians didn't know a question-- the teacher ask the Jew. If the Jew didn't know, the Jew got a bad grade. You got the bad grade, you were flunking out of school. Then when you came out, you know, you had limericks, degrading limericks verses and stones thrown after you.
